title: Linear Search (O(n))
code: static int LinearSearch(int[] nums, int target)

frames.caption: Scan left to right for 23. No structure is assumed — the array need not be sorted, so every cell is a candidate until checked.
frames.note: Linear search is the baseline every other search is measured against. It makes no assumption about the data (unlike binary search, which demands sorted input), so it always works — but that generality costs a full pass. We track a "comparisons" counter so the O(n) cost is visible, not just asserted.

frames.caption: i=0: nums[0] = 4 ≠ 23 — no match, advance. (comparisons = 1)
frames.note: This is the one unit of work that defines O(n): a single comparison of nums[i] against the target. Watch the comparisons counter — it ticks once per element, so total work grows in direct proportion to how far the target sits from the front. That straight-line growth ("about one unit per element") is exactly what "linear time" means.

frames.caption: i=1: nums[1] = 8 ≠ 23 — no match, advance. (comparisons = 2)
frames.note: This is the one unit of work that defines O(n): a single comparison of nums[i] against the target. Watch the comparisons counter — it ticks once per element, so total work grows in direct proportion to how far the target sits from the front. That straight-line growth ("about one unit per element") is exactly what "linear time" means.

frames.caption: i=2: nums[2] = 15 ≠ 23 — no match, advance. (comparisons = 3)
frames.note: This is the one unit of work that defines O(n): a single comparison of nums[i] against the target. Watch the comparisons counter — it ticks once per element, so total work grows in direct proportion to how far the target sits from the front. That straight-line growth ("about one unit per element") is exactly what "linear time" means.

frames.caption: i=3: nums[3] = 16 ≠ 23 — no match, advance. (comparisons = 4)
frames.note: This is the one unit of work that defines O(n): a single comparison of nums[i] against the target. Watch the comparisons counter — it ticks once per element, so total work grows in direct proportion to how far the target sits from the front. That straight-line growth ("about one unit per element") is exactly what "linear time" means.

frames.caption: i=4: nums[4] = 23 == 23 — found it after 5 comparisons. Return 4.
frames.note: A match ends the scan immediately — that early return is why the BEST case is O(1) (target at index 0) while the WORST case is O(n) (target last or absent). Here the target sat near the end, so it took 5 of 6 comparisons; the AVERAGE over random positions is about n/2, still Θ(n). Contrast binary search: on SORTED data it discards half the range per probe and finds the answer in ~log₂(n) ≈ 3 comparisons instead of 5 — the payoff for paying the sort cost up front.
